stem:
You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman self-referred for assessment after her 6-year-old child was diagnosed with ADHD. She reports lifelong difficulty sustaining attention at school and work, forgetfulness with deadlines, losing belongings, procrastination, frequent task-switching, restlessness, and impulsive decisions. She failed to complete two TAFE courses despite high IQ. Symptoms are present at work, at home, and in her relationship.

She was always called daydreamy at primary school but had no school disciplinary issues. School reports from age 8 confirm distractibility, untidy work, and frequent careless mistakes.

data_table.Examination: Normal mental state: euthymic, no psychomotor abnormality beyond restlessness, no psychotic features, no anxiety beyond performance-related
data_table.Investigations: K10 score 14 (mild distress, non-clinical); ASRS-V1.1 part A 5 of 6 positive; collateral from mother confirms childhood symptoms; school reports from age 8 documenting inattentive features; no significant developmental, perinatal, or head injury history

lead_in: What is the most appropriate basis for diagnosing adult ADHD in this patient?

options.text: Adult onset symptoms within the last 12 months are sufficient to diagnose ADHD if 5 of 9 criteria are met
options.rationale: DSM-5 requires several symptoms present BEFORE age 12 (not necessarily impairing) plus current persistence; truly adult-onset presentation without childhood symptoms is not ADHD by current criteria. (Source: DSM-5; Australian ADHD Guideline)

options.text: At least 5 of 9 inattentive or hyperactive symptoms in adults, with several symptoms before age 12 across settings
options.rationale: DSM-5 adult ADHD: >=5 of 9 inattentive AND/OR hyperactive-impulsive symptoms (vs 6 in children), several symptoms present before age 12, symptoms in 2 or more settings, impairing function, not better explained by another disorder. (Source: DSM-5; Australian ADHD Guideline)

options.text: Positive Conners Adult ADHD Rating Scale alone is sufficient to confirm ADHD diagnosis in primary care
options.rationale: Rating scales (Conners, ASRS) screen but do not replace structured clinical interview, collateral history, and DSM-5 criteria assessment for diagnosis. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline)

options.text: Neuropsychological testing showing impaired sustained attention is required for adult ADHD diagnostic confirmation
options.rationale: Neuropsychological tests are supportive but not required; many patients with ADHD have normal continuous performance test results due to compensation strategies. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline)

options.text: All 9 inattentive or all 9 hyperactive symptoms must be present in adulthood to meet diagnostic threshold
options.rationale: Threshold is 5 of 9 in adults (DSM-5 lowered from 6 of 9 for children to reflect symptom attenuation with maturation). (Source: DSM-5)

explanation.summary: DSM-5 adult ADHD criteria require >=5 of 9 inattentive AND/OR >=5 of 9 hyperactive-impulsive symptoms (vs 6 of 9 in children under 17 to reflect symptom attenuation), with several symptoms present before age 12 (not necessarily impairing), symptoms in >=2 settings (work, home, social, study), clear impairment of social/academic/occupational function, and not better explained by another mental disorder. Three presentations: inattentive, hyperactive-impulsive, combined. Adult women often present with predominantly inattentive symptoms missed in childhood (daydreaminess vs disruptive hyperactivity in boys); diagnosis often follows child diagnosis. The Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD (AADPA 2022) recommends structured clinical interview using DSM-5 plus validated rating scales (ASRS, Conners), collateral history (parent, partner, school reports), screening for comorbid mood/anxiety, sleep, substance use, autism, and learning disorders. Pharmacotherapy is methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine first-line; atomoxetine non-stimulant alternative; behavioural therapy and coaching adjunctive.
key_points: DSM-5 adult: >=5 of 9 inattentive AND/OR >=5 hyperactive-impulsive
Several symptoms before age 12 required (not necessarily impairing)
Symptoms in >=2 settings, clear impairment, not better explained
Women often missed: inattentive presentation, daydreamy phenotype
Australian AADPA guideline + DSM-5 + collateral history + rating scales
explanation.pearls: Adult ADHD prevalence in Australia is approximately 2 to 5%, persisting from childhood in about 50% of paediatric cases; lifetime stimulant exposure does not increase substance use disorder risk and may reduce it in treated populations.

stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 32-year-old man brought by ambulance 90 minutes after intentional overdose of his partner's amitriptyline (estimated 50 tablets of 25 mg, so 1250 mg) found beside an empty wine bottle. He is drowsy (GCS 12), tachycardic, and hypotensive. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 132 bpm with QRS duration of 130 ms and terminal R wave in lead aVR of 4 mm. VBG shows pH 7.28, lactate 3.1, HCO3 18. He has not had a seizure yet. He is intubated for airway protection.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/56, HR 132, RR 22 (intubated and ventilated), SpO2 99%, T 37.0, GCS 12 before intubation
data_table.Examination: Dilated pupils 6 mm sluggishly reactive, dry mucous membranes, urinary retention, no focal neurology before sedation, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia 132, QRS 130 ms, terminal R wave aVR 4 mm, QTc 480; VBG: pH 7.28, HCO3 18, lactate 3.1, K 3.6, glucose 7.2; troponin pending; paracetamol level negative
data_table.Medications: (Partner) amitriptyline 25 mg nocte; patient on no regular medications

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?

options.text: IV flumazenil 200 mcg titrated up to 1 mg total as reversal for sedation effects with continued ECG monitoring
options.rationale: Flumazenil is benzodiazepine antidote, irrelevant to TCA; also precipitates seizures in TCA overdose and is contraindicated. (Source: Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 2 g loading then 2 g/hour infusion for prolonged QTc and risk of torsades de pointes
options.rationale: Magnesium is given for documented torsades but is not the priority intervention for sodium channel blockade with widened QRS; bicarbonate addresses the primary toxicity. (Source: Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: IV intralipid 20% 1.5 mL/kg bolus then infusion at 0.25 mL/kg/min for lipid soluble TCA overdose
options.rationale: Intralipid is reserved for cardiac arrest or refractory haemodynamic collapse with TCA; not first-line before bicarbonate trial. (Source: Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: IV physostigmine 1 mg titrated as antidote for anticholinergic toxidrome to reverse neurological effects rapidly
options.rationale: Physostigmine is CONTRAINDICATED in TCA overdose due to risk of asystole, seizures, and worsening conduction; never used in this setting. (Source: Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol (8.4%) bolus then infusion targeting QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55
options.rationale: QRS >100 ms, terminal R in aVR >3 mm, hypotension and acidosis are all indications for sodium bicarbonate in TCA overdose; mechanism is sodium loading (sodium channel blockade reversal) and alkalinisation (decreases protein binding, free drug); target pH 7.45-7.55. (Source: ANZICS Tox Guidelines; Poisons Information Centre)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes cardiotoxicity through sodium channel blockade (QRS widening, terminal R wave in aVR, ventricular arrhythmia), potassium channel blockade (QTc prolongation), alpha-1 blockade (hypotension), and anticholinergic toxidrome (dilated pupils, dry skin, urinary retention, tachycardia, ileus, agitation, hyperthermia). Mortality risk markers: QRS >100 ms (seizure risk), QRS >160 ms (ventricular arrhythmia risk), terminal R wave aVR >3 mm. Management: airway protection (intubate for GCS <12 or seizures); IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus then infusion to target pH 7.45 to 7.55 - sodium loads the channel and alkalinisation decreases protein binding and free active drug; benzodiazepines for seizures and agitation; vasopressors (noradrenaline) for refractory hypotension; intralipid for cardiac arrest. Activated charcoal within 1 to 2 hours if airway protected. Physostigmine and flumazenil are CONTRAINDICATED. ICU admission for at least 24 hours; QRS normalisation precedes safe discharge.
key_points: TCA cardiotoxicity: QRS widening, terminal R aVR, QTc long, hypotension
Bicarbonate indications: QRS >100, terminal R aVR >3 mm, hypotension, acidosis
Target pH 7.45-7.55 with bicarbonate bolus + infusion
Physostigmine and flumazenil CONTRAINDICATED
Intralipid reserved for cardiac arrest or refractory shock
explanation.pearls: Terminal R wave in lead aVR >3 mm has approximately 80% sensitivity and 75% specificity for predicting seizure or arrhythmia in TCA overdose, and triggers immediate sodium bicarbonate even when QRS is borderline at 100 to 120 ms.

subtopic_detail: Valproate use in women of childbearing potential
stem: You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 27-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder who has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 4 years following 2 previous severe manic episodes requiring inpatient admission. She has a new long-term partner and tells you they are planning to start a family within the next 12 months. She has had no further mood episodes on valproate. Her contraception is condoms only. Her mother also had bipolar disorder. She asks about continuing her medication during a planned pregnancy and breastfeeding.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: euthymic, no psychomotor abnormality, no psychotic features
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 540 micromol/L (therapeutic); FBC, LFTs, EUC normal; pregnancy test today negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her management?

options.text: Continue valproate at lowest effective dose with high-dose folate 5 mg daily commenced 3 months pre-conception
options.rationale: Valproate teratogenicity (NTD risk 1-2%, IQ loss about 7 to 10 points, ADHD/ASD risk) cannot be mitigated by folate; valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy planning unless no alternative. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention; AMH)

options.text: Switch to carbamazepine 400 mg twice daily for safer pregnancy and breastfeeding profile than valproate
options.rationale: Carbamazepine also has substantial teratogenicity (NTD risk about 1%, similar developmental risks); lithium or lamotrigine are preferred alternatives in pregnancy. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guideline)

options.text: Switch to lithium with serum-level monitoring monthly antenatally and consider TFTs 3-monthly during pregnancy
options.rationale: Lithium is preferred mood stabiliser in pregnancy for bipolar maintenance (lower teratogenicity than valproate; Ebstein anomaly relative risk historically overstated, actual risk about 0.6% with first-trimester exposure); monthly levels (rising volume of distribution), TFTs 3-monthly, foetal echo at 16 to 20 weeks. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guideline; TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Cease all mood stabilisers and rely on close clinical monitoring with rapid restart of valproate if relapse occurs
options.rationale: Discontinuation of mood stabiliser in stable bipolar I carries 50-70% relapse risk; pregnancy and postpartum are high-risk periods for relapse, so maintenance is required. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guideline)

options.text: Continue valproate, ensure long-acting reversible contraception, and counsel against pregnancy with both partners
options.rationale: This patient has expressed clear intent to conceive; appropriate response is medication switch to a safer alternative rather than denying her reproductive autonomy. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

explanation.summary: Valproate is highly teratogenic with major malformation rate of 10% (vs 2 to 3% background), neural tube defects 1 to 2%, mean IQ loss 7 to 10 points, and substantially increased risk of autism spectrum disorder and ADHD in exposed offspring. The TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program (and equivalent EMA program) mandates: avoid in women of childbearing potential unless no effective alternative AND patient is on highly effective contraception (LARC preferred) AND annual specialist review using risk acknowledgement form AND patient information document signed. For bipolar I maintenance in women planning pregnancy, switch options include lithium (preferred, monitoring intensified during pregnancy), lamotrigine (excellent pregnancy profile, less effective for mania prevention), or atypical antipsychotic (quetiapine, olanzapine). Lithium pregnancy management: monthly levels (rising volume of distribution; lithium clearance increases in pregnancy then falls postpartum), TFTs every trimester, foetal echo at 16 to 20 weeks (Ebstein anomaly risk about 0.6%), continue throughout pregnancy at lowest effective dose, hold during labour or reduce dose 24 to 48 hours pre-delivery, restart with reduced dose immediately postpartum.
key_points: Valproate teratogenicity: 10% major malformations, NTD 1-2%, IQ -7 to -10
TGA Pregnancy Prevention Program mandatory for valproate in WOCBP
Alternatives: lithium (preferred), lamotrigine, atypical antipsychotic
Lithium pregnancy: monthly levels, TFTs/trimester, foetal echo 16-20 weeks
Postpartum is highest risk for bipolar relapse; maintain mood stabiliser
explanation.pearls: Valproate first-trimester exposure carries a neural tube defect risk of approximately 1 to 2%, which is 10 times the background population rate, and is not preventable by even high-dose 5 mg folate.

subtopic_detail: DOAC choice in AF with renal impairment
stem: You are the cardiology registrar reviewing a 78-year-old woman with newly diagnosed permanent atrial fibrillation found on ambulatory monitoring after dizziness. She has type 2 diabetes, hypertension, prior TIA 2 years ago, and is taking metformin, perindopril, and aspirin. Her CHA2DS2-VA score is 5. Her creatinine clearance (Cockcroft-Gault) is 38 mL/min. She has no history of GI bleeding, no liver disease, no drug interactions, and weighs 62 kg. She has reasonable oral intake and good adherence to medications historically.

data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, no murmurs, no signs of heart failure, no carotid bruits
data_table.Investigations: ECG: AF rate 88, no ischaemic changes; FBC: Hb 124, plt 240; EUC: Cr 132, eGFR 38, CrCl 38; LFTs normal; HbA1c 7.2%
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD, perindopril 5 mg daily, aspirin 100 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation choice?

options.text: Continue aspirin 100 mg daily without anticoagulation given her age and the bleeding risk this carries
options.rationale: Aspirin is not effective stroke prevention in AF and is no longer recommended; CHA2DS2-VA 5 with high stroke risk mandates anticoagulation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Warfarin with INR target 2.0 to 3.0 monitored monthly because DOACs are contraindicated below CrCl 50
options.rationale: DOACs are NOT contraindicated at CrCl 38; apixaban and rivaroxaban have approved dosing down to CrCl 15 to 30 with dose reduction; warfarin is acceptable but not preferred. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily continued with aspirin 100 mg daily for secondary prevention of stroke and TIA
options.rationale: Adding aspirin to oral anticoagulation in AF (without recent ACS or PCI) increases major bleeding without reducing stroke; aspirin should be ceased on starting DOAC. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily with cessation of aspirin in view of bleeding risk on combined antithrombotic therapy
options.rationale: Apixaban 5 mg BD is correct here: dose reduction requires 2 of 3 criteria (age >=80, weight <=60 kg, Cr >=133); she meets 0 to 1 (Cr 132 borderline; age 78 <80; weight 62 >60). Aspirin must be ceased to avoid net bleeding harm; apixaban has best bleeding profile among DOACs especially in CKD and elderly. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Dabigatran 150 mg twice daily with cessation of aspirin once anticoagulation is established at steady state
options.rationale: Dabigatran requires dose reduction (110 mg BD) for age >=75 and is renally cleared (80%); contraindicated below CrCl 30 and risky between 30 and 50; apixaban is safer in CKD. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

explanation.summary: NHFA/CSANZ AF guideline: CHA2DS2-VA (Victorian replacement of CHA2DS2-VASc, no sex category) >=2 in men or women is an indication for oral anticoagulation. DOACs are first-line over warfarin for non-valvular AF. Apixaban 5 mg BD (or 2.5 mg BD if 2 of: age >=80, weight <=60 kg, Cr >=133 micromol/L), rivaroxaban 20 mg daily (15 mg if CrCl 15 to 50), dabigatran 150 mg BD (110 mg BD if age >=75 or bleeding risk; contraindicated CrCl <30), edoxaban 60 mg daily (30 mg if CrCl 15 to 50). Warfarin remains preferred for mechanical valves, moderate to severe mitral stenosis, antiphospholipid syndrome, end-stage renal disease. Aspirin should be ceased on starting DOAC unless concurrent recent ACS within 12 months. HAS-BLED guides bleeding risk discussion but is rarely a reason to withhold anticoagulation when stroke risk is high. Annual review of renal function (more often if CKD), weight, age, and bleeding events.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VA >=2: anticoagulate (DOAC first-line)
Apixaban dose reduction needs 2 of 3: age >=80, weight <=60, Cr >=133
Dabigatran contraindicated CrCl <30; rivaroxaban/edoxaban reduce at CrCl 15-50
Aspirin offers no stroke benefit in AF and should be ceased on DOAC
Warfarin reserved for mechanical valves, moderate-severe MS, APS
explanation.pearls: Apixaban demonstrated lower major bleeding (especially intracranial haemorrhage) than warfarin in ARISTOTLE, including in patients with CKD and the elderly, making it the preferred DOAC in renal impairment down to CrCl 25 mL/min.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a previously well 44-year-old man with 4 days of productive cough with green sputum, fevers to 38.6, and pleuritic right-sided chest pain. He has no known comorbidities, takes no regular medications, and has no penicillin allergy. He is tolerating oral fluids and food. CXR shows right lower lobe consolidation. His SMART-COP score is 1 (multilobar = 0, albumin not done, RR 22 = 0, tachycardia 96 = 0, confusion 0, oxygen 0, pH not done; only SBP 95 which is 1 from threshold; actually SBP 118 so 0). Current vitals are within near-normal range otherwise.

data_table.Examination: Bronchial breath sounds right base with crepitations, dullness to percussion, no signs of effusion, no tracheal deviation, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: CXR: right lower lobe consolidation; no bloods needed in primary care setting; SMART-COP estimated score 0; not assessing for severe pneumonia by Australian guideline

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily for 5 days with safety net and review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Mild CAP (SMART-COP 0 to 2) managed in community: amoxicillin 1 g TDS for 5 days is Australian first-line per eTG; reserve doxycycline for atypical concern or amoxicillin allergy; combination therapy is for moderate to severe CAP. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; eTG)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Augmentin + doxycycline is moderate-severity CAP regimen; SMART-COP 0 mild CAP does not need this; broader spectrum is unnecessary. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admission for IV benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 6 hourly plus IV doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: IV admission is reserved for moderate to severe CAP (SMART-COP >=3) or social/medical factors preventing oral therapy; not indicated here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral azithromycin 500 mg daily for 3 days as monotherapy targeting atypical pneumonia pathogens primarily
options.rationale: Macrolide monotherapy is not Australian first-line for CAP due to increasing pneumococcal resistance; reserved for atypical-only presentations. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral moxifloxacin 400 mg daily for 7 days as monotherapy covering typical and atypical organisms equally
options.rationale: Respiratory fluoroquinolone is reserved for severe penicillin allergy or specific atypical or resistant infections; routine first-line is amoxicillin. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: SMART-COP stratifies CAP severity in Australia: Systolic BP <90 (2), Multilobar infiltrate (1), Albumin <35 (1), Respiratory rate >=25 (under 50 = 1, over 50 = 2), Tachycardia >=125 (1), Confusion new (1), Oxygen low (under 50 yo SpO2 <94 or PaO2 <70 = 2; over 50 yo SpO2 <90 or PaO2 <60 = 2), pH <7.35 (2). Score 0 to 2 mild (community oral); 3 to 4 moderate (admit, IV antibiotic); >=5 severe (ICU consideration). Mild CAP empirical: amoxicillin 1 g TDS oral for 5 days. Moderate CAP: IV benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 6-hourly + oral doxycycline 100 mg BD (or oral azithromycin); switch to oral when stable. Severe CAP: IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily + IV azithromycin 500 mg daily +/- oseltamivir in influenza season; ICU consultation. Mycoplasma and Chlamydia coverage with doxycycline or macrolide when atypical features (younger patient, dry cough, extrapulmonary manifestations). Follow-up CXR at 6 weeks to confirm resolution and exclude underlying malignancy especially in smokers over 50.
key_points: SMART-COP: severity stratification (0-2 mild, 3-4 mod, >=5 severe)
Mild oral: amoxicillin 1 g TDS x 5 days
Moderate: IV benzylpenicillin + oral doxycycline
Severe: IV ceftriaxone + IV azithromycin + ICU consult
Repeat CXR at 6 weeks especially in smokers >50
explanation.pearls: Australian eTG shortened first-line amoxicillin duration from 7 to 5 days for mild CAP based on the OAR (Outpatient Antibiotic Reduction) and ANTIC pneumonia trials showing non-inferior outcomes and reduced selection pressure.

stem:
You are the GP registrar reviewing a 68-year-old postmenopausal woman with a recent low-trauma fracture of the left distal radius from a fall at standing height. She has no other fractures and no back pain. DXA scan today: lumbar spine T-score -2.8, total hip T-score -2.5, femoral neck T-score -2.7. Vitamin D is 78 nmol/L (adequate). Calcium and PTH normal.

eGFR 64 (mildly reduced). No history of dental disease, no planned dental procedures, no oesophageal disease, and no upper GI symptoms. She is otherwise healthy.

data_table.Examination: Healing distal radius fracture in cast, no kyphosis, no other tender vertebrae
data_table.Investigations: DXA: LS T -2.8, hip T -2.5, FN T -2.7; vitamin D 78; calcium 2.32; PTH 4.2; eGFR 64; TSH 1.8; coeliac screen negative; FBC normal; SPEP normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line osteoporosis therapy?

options.text: Annual IV zoledronic acid 5 mg infusion as first-line therapy without trial of oral therapy initially
options.rationale: IV zoledronic acid is reserved for oral bisphosphonate intolerance, upper GI contraindication, or adherence concerns; oral alendronate is first-line. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

options.text: Subcutaneous denosumab 60 mg every 6 months for postmenopausal osteoporosis with vertebral fracture prevention
options.rationale: Denosumab is an acceptable alternative but is reserved as second-line for intolerance or contraindication to bisphosphonates per PBS criteria; oral alendronate is preferred first-line. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

options.text: Subcutaneous teriparatide 20 mcg daily for 18 months as anabolic therapy for severe postmenopausal osteoporosis
options.rationale: Teriparatide is reserved for severe osteoporosis (T-score <-3.0 with multiple vertebral fractures) under specialist supervision; not first-line. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

options.text: Oral raloxifene 60 mg daily as selective oestrogen receptor modulator for postmenopausal osteoporosis treatment
options.rationale: Raloxifene is third-line, reduces vertebral but not hip fractures, and carries VTE risk; not first-line in 68-year-old with hip T-score -2.5. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline)

options.text: Oral alendronate 70 mg weekly with calcium and vitamin D continued and 3-yearly DXA monitoring arranged
options.rationale: Oral bisphosphonate (alendronate or risedronate) is Australian first-line for postmenopausal osteoporosis with prior fragility fracture or T-score <=-2.5; PBS criteria met; no contraindications; eGFR 64 (>35 required for bisphosphonates). (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guideline; AMH)

explanation.summary: Australian osteoporosis treatment indications: T-score <=-2.5 at any site, or prior minimal trauma vertebral or hip fracture (regardless of T-score), or T-score -1.5 to -2.5 with FRAX 10-year hip fracture risk >=3% or major osteoporotic risk >=20%. First-line oral bisphosphonate (alendronate 70 mg weekly or risedronate 35 mg weekly) - 50% reduction in vertebral fractures and 40% reduction in hip fractures. Pre-treatment: dental check (BRONJ prevention), correct vitamin D and calcium, exclude secondary causes (multiple myeloma, hyperthyroidism, coeliac, hyperparathyroidism, Cushing). Contraindications: eGFR <35, oesophageal disease (oral), recent or planned invasive dental work (relative). Alternatives: IV zoledronic acid annually (oral intolerance), denosumab 60 mg SC 6-monthly (CKD-compatible but rebound vertebral fracture risk on cessation), teriparatide (severe), romosozumab (very severe), raloxifene (third-line). DXA review at 2 to 3 years. Drug holiday after 5 years oral or 3 years IV in low-risk patients; continue indefinitely if high risk.
key_points: Treat: T <=-2.5, OR prior fragility fracture, OR T -1.5 to -2.5 + high FRAX
First-line: oral alendronate or risedronate (50% vertebral, 40% hip fracture reduction)
Pre-treat: dental check, vitamin D, exclude secondary causes
Contraindications oral: eGFR <35, oesophageal disease
Drug holiday consideration at 5 years oral or 3 years IV
explanation.pearls: Denosumab cessation carries a 10-fold rebound vertebral fracture risk within 12 months if not transitioned to a bisphosphonate, making it a less reversible choice than oral alendronate for patients who may discontinue therapy.

stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman with known mild asthma on PRN salbutamol who presents with 12 hours of worsening dyspnoea, wheeze, and cough after a viral URTI. She has used salbutamol 6 puffs hourly without sustained improvement. She is sitting forward, speaking in short phrases of 4 to 5 words. She has not been intubated or admitted to ICU for asthma previously and has had no prior systemic steroid course this year. There are no triggers other than the recent URTI.

data_table.Examination: Sitting forward, accessory muscle use, audible wheeze, prolonged expiratory phase, equal air entry, no focal consolidation, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: PEF 38% of predicted before treatment; arterial blood gas: pH 7.40, PaCO2 38, PaO2 64, HCO3 22; CXR: clear, no consolidation, no pneumothorax

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Salbutamol 5 mg + ipratropium 500 mcg nebulised, oxygen to maintain SpO2 93 to 95, oral prednisolone 50 mg now
options.rationale: Severe asthma (PEF 33 to 50%, RR >=25, HR >=110, unable to speak in full sentences) requires combined nebulised salbutamol + ipratropium, targeted oxygen (SpO2 93-95 to avoid hyperoxia worsening V/Q), and early systemic steroid (oral prednisolone equivalent to IV); reassess at 15 to 30 minutes. (Source: Asthma Australia; National Asthma Council)

options.text: High-flow nasal oxygen at 60 L/min FiO2 1.0 then salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer hourly with reassessment in 1 hour
options.rationale: FiO2 1.0 risks hypercapnic worsening in severe asthma; target SpO2 is 93 to 95%; spacer salbutamol alone undertreats severe exacerbation without ipratropium or systemic steroid. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg as initial pharmacotherapy for severe airway obstruction without delay for cannulation
options.rationale: IM adrenaline is for anaphylaxis, not asthma exacerbation; reserved for asthma with anaphylactic features or impending arrest. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Intubation and mechanical ventilation immediately under rapid sequence induction without pre-treatment trial
options.rationale: She is severe but not life-threatening (no silent chest, no exhaustion, normal CO2); medical therapy trial is appropriate before intubation. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 2 g infusion over 20 minutes as first-line bronchodilator without nebulised therapy first
options.rationale: IV magnesium is added if no response to first-line nebulised therapy + steroid at 30 to 60 minutes; not initial treatment. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

explanation.summary: Australian acute asthma severity: mild-moderate (PEF >50%, full sentences, normal mental state, RR <25, HR <110); severe (PEF 33 to 50%, phrases not sentences, RR >=25, HR >=110); life-threatening (silent chest, exhaustion, cyanosis, hypotension, altered consciousness, PEF <33%, normal or raised PaCO2). Treatment: oxygen titrated to SpO2 93-95%; salbutamol nebulised 5 mg (or via spacer if mild-moderate); ipratropium 500 mcg nebulised added for severe; systemic corticosteroid (oral prednisolone 50 mg or IV hydrocortisone 100 mg if vomiting); reassess at 15 to 30 minutes. Step up to IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes if poor response. ICU consult for life-threatening features, rising PaCO2, or fatigue. Avoid sedatives. Salbutamol can cause lactic acidosis and hypokalaemia; check VBG and K. Discharge criteria: PEF >70%, sustained off bronchodilator for 4 hours; 5-day oral prednisolone 50 mg daily; written asthma action plan; ICS commencement or step-up if not already; GP/respiratory follow-up.
key_points: Severe: PEF 33-50%, phrases, RR>=25, HR>=110
First-line severe: nebulised salbutamol + ipratropium + steroid + targeted O2
Target SpO2 93-95% (avoid hyperoxia worsening V/Q)
IV magnesium 2 g if poor response at 30-60 minutes
Discharge: 5d prednisolone, written action plan, ICS step-up
explanation.pearls: Salbutamol-induced lactic acidosis and hypokalaemia are dose-dependent and common after continuous nebulisation; check VBG and replace potassium when K <3.5 to avoid arrhythmia, while continuing therapy.

subtopic_detail: Acute aortic dissection initial medical therapy
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 62-year-old man who presents with sudden onset tearing interscapular chest and back pain 90 minutes ago, radiating to the abdomen. He has long-standing poorly controlled hypertension. He looks unwell and diaphoretic. He is hypertensive with 30 mmHg difference between arms. CT aortogram shows a type B (descending) aortic dissection extending from the left subclavian origin to the iliac bifurcation, with no malperfusion of visceral or renal branches and no rupture or haemothorax. He is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 192/108 right arm, 162/96 left arm, HR 96, RR 22, SpO2 96% RA, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, equal but reduced left radial pulse, tender thoracic spine, abdomen soft, no peritonism, neurologically intact
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus rhythm, no ischaemic changes; CXR: mediastinal widening; CT aortogram: type B dissection L subclavian to iliac bifurcation, no malperfusion, no rupture; troponin negative; D-dimer markedly raised
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily (poor adherence)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate medical therapy?

options.text: IV labetalol infusion titrated to systolic BP 100 to 120 mmHg and heart rate under 60 with continuous arterial line
options.rationale: Acute aortic dissection requires rapid BP control AND heart rate control (rate-pressure product reduction); IV labetalol (combined alpha and beta blockade) is Australian first-line, targeting HR <60 and SBP 100 to 120 (lowest tolerating end-organ perfusion); arterial line for continuous monitoring; rate control must precede vasodilator therapy. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection; ESC Aortic)

options.text: IV hydralazine 10 mg boluses repeated every 30 minutes targeting systolic blood pressure 130 mmHg ongoing
options.rationale: Hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia which increases dP/dt and propagates dissection; pure vasodilator without rate control is contraindicated as initial therapy. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

options.text: IV sodium nitroprusside infusion targeting systolic blood pressure 110 mmHg without beta blockade now
options.rationale: Nitroprusside is potent vasodilator but causes reflex tachycardia; if used, must be combined with beta blocker first; not standalone first-line. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Urgent surgical consultation with view to endovascular stent grafting now before pharmacological BP control
options.rationale: Type B dissection without malperfusion or rupture is managed medically initially (Best Medical Therapy); endovascular repair reserved for complications; medical BP control still first priority. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

options.text: IV sublingual nifedipine 10 mg titrated to systolic blood pressure 130 with reassessment every 15 minutes
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes uncontrolled hypotension and reflex tachycardia and is contraindicated; smooth controlled IV titration is required. (Source: NHFA Aortic Dissection)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection is classified by Stanford: type A (involves ascending aorta) - surgical emergency; type B (descending only, distal to left subclavian) - medical management unless complicated. DeBakey I (asc + arch + desc), II (asc only), III (desc only). Medical therapy targets reduction of dP/dt (rate of pressure change) by lowering BP AND heart rate simultaneously - shear stress propagates dissection. Australian first-line: IV labetalol (combined alpha-beta) infusion titrated to HR <60 and SBP 100 to 120; alternatives are IV esmolol (very short half-life) plus IV nitroprusside or GTN. Rate control MUST precede vasodilator (pure vasodilator alone increases dP/dt via reflex tachycardia). Type A: emergency cardiothoracic surgery (mortality 1 to 2% per hour untreated). Complicated type B (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain or BP, rapid expansion): TEVAR (thoracic endovascular aortic repair) at vascular surgery centre. Uncomplicated type B: ICU/HDU medical therapy with serial imaging; lifelong intensive BP control after discharge.
key_points: Stanford A (asc): surgery emergency; B (desc): medical if uncomplicated
Reduce dP/dt: target HR <60 + SBP 100-120
IV labetalol first-line (combined alpha-beta)
Rate control MUST precede vasodilator
TEVAR for complicated B: malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain
explanation.pearls: Untreated type A aortic dissection has a mortality of approximately 1 to 2% per hour in the first 24 to 48 hours, which is why CT aortogram must be expedited and cardiothoracic surgery contacted before transfer rather than after.

stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 58-year-old woman with severe headache, blurred vision, and nausea over 4 hours. She has known hypertension on irbesartan with poor adherence. Examination shows BP 226/132, papilloedema, and grade 3 hypertensive retinopathy. She has no focal neurological deficit. Urinalysis: protein +++, blood +. Bloods show acute kidney injury (Cr 168 from baseline 88), microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia features (schistocytes, low platelets, raised LDH), but troponin and ECG are normal. CT brain shows no acute stroke, no haemorrhage. She is alert and oriented.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 226/132 (both arms), HR 104, T 36.7, RR 18, SpO2 98% RA
data_table.Examination: Bilateral papilloedema, flame haemorrhages and cotton-wool spots on fundoscopy, no focal neurology, no chest pain, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Cr 168 (baseline 88), eGFR 32, Hb 96 with schistocytes on film, plt 88, LDH 1240, haptoglobin <0.1, urinalysis: protein +++, blood +; ECG and troponin normal; CT brain clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate BP target and rate of reduction over the first hour?

options.text: Reduce blood pressure to 200/110 over the first hour using sublingual nifedipine to avoid IV access and admission
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes uncontrolled hypotension and reflex tachycardia; contraindicated in hypertensive emergency; IV titration is required. (Source: ESC Hypertension)

options.text: Reduce blood pressure to 140/90 within the first 30 minutes using IV labetalol bolus and continuous infusion
options.rationale: Rapid reduction to near-normal risks watershed cerebral infarction in chronically autoregulated patients; 20 to 25% MAP reduction in first hour is the safe target. (Source: ESC Hypertension)

options.text: Reduce blood pressure to under 110/70 within 6 hours using IV sodium nitroprusside continuous infusion
options.rationale: Targeting below 110/70 risks malperfusion of chronically autoregulated brain, kidney, and coronary beds; nitroprusside risks cyanide toxicity in renal impairment. (Source: ESC Hypertension)

options.text: Reduce blood pressure to under 180/110 over 24 hours using oral amlodipine and oral hydralazine combination
options.rationale: Oral therapy is too slow for hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage; IV titrated agent required to gain hour-by-hour control. (Source: ESC Hypertension)

options.text: Reduce mean arterial pressure by no more than 20 to 25% over the first hour with IV labetalol or IV nicardipine titrated
options.rationale: Hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage (encephalopathy, malignant hypertension with retinopathy, AKI, MAHA): reduce MAP by 20 to 25% over first hour, then to 160/100 to 110 over next 2 to 6 hours, then to normal over 24 to 48 hours; IV labetalol or nicardipine; faster reduction risks watershed infarction. (Source: ESC Hypertension; AAFP)

explanation.summary: Hypertensive emergency = severe BP elevation (typically SBP >180 or DBP >120) with acute target organ damage: hypertensive encephalopathy, acute stroke (ischaemic or haemorrhagic), pulmonary oedema, acute coronary syndrome, aortic dissection, AKI, eclampsia, microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (malignant hypertension). Hypertensive urgency = same BP without end-organ damage; managed with oral therapy and outpatient review. Emergency target: reduce MAP by no more than 20 to 25% over the first hour, to 160/100 to 110 over next 2 to 6 hours, then to normal over 24 to 48 hours. Exception: aortic dissection requires more aggressive reduction (target SBP 100 to 120 within 20 minutes); ischaemic stroke for thrombolysis target <185/110; haemorrhagic stroke target SBP <140; eclampsia target diastolic 90 to 100. IV agents: labetalol (broadly applicable), nicardipine (dihydropyridine CCB), GTN (LV failure, ACS), nitroprusside (rarely used now, cyanide risk in renal failure), hydralazine (used in eclampsia). Avoid: sublingual nifedipine (no longer recommended), clonidine for rapid lowering. Investigate secondary causes (renal artery stenosis, phaeochromocytoma, primary aldosteronism) once stable.
key_points: Emergency: severe BP + target organ damage (CNS, cardiac, renal, MAHA)
First hour: reduce MAP by max 20-25%; over-rapid risks watershed infarction
Then to 160/100-110 over 2-6 hours, normal over 24-48 hours
Aortic dissection exception: SBP 100-120 within 20 min
Sublingual nifedipine contraindicated in modern practice
explanation.pearls: Malignant hypertension is diagnosed by severely elevated BP with papilloedema (grade 4 hypertensive retinopathy) or flame haemorrhages and cotton-wool spots (grade 3), and untreated 1-year mortality is approximately 80%; aggressive yet controlled BP reduction reduces this to under 10%.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 64-year-old man with progressive dyspnoea over 6 weeks and 4 kg weight loss. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and worked in shipbuilding in his 20s. CXR shows a large right pleural effusion. Diagnostic thoracentesis yields straw-coloured fluid. Results: pleural protein 35 g/L, serum protein 62 g/L; pleural LDH 280 U/L, serum LDH 220 U/L (ULN serum 230). Pleural glucose 4.0 mmol/L. Pleural pH 7.34. Cytology and TB culture pending. CT chest reports right pleural thickening with nodularity.

data_table.Examination: Dullness to percussion right base to mid-zone, absent breath sounds right base, reduced vocal resonance, no clubbing, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Pleural fluid: protein 35 g/L, LDH 280 U/L, glucose 4.0, pH 7.34; serum: protein 62 g/L, LDH 220 U/L, albumin 32; FBC normal; CT chest: right pleural thickening with nodularity, no parenchymal lesion

lead_in: How should this pleural effusion be characterised and what is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Transudative effusion most likely due to congestive cardiac failure; manage with diuretic and outpatient review
options.rationale: Light criteria not met for transudate: pleural/serum protein ratio 0.56 (>0.5) and pleural/serum LDH ratio 1.27 (>0.6); this is exudative. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guideline)

options.text: Exudative effusion meeting Light criteria; arrange CT-guided pleural biopsy or thoracoscopy for mesothelioma exclusion
options.rationale: Pleural/serum protein 0.56 (>0.5) AND LDH ratio 1.27 (>0.6) AND pleural LDH >2/3 ULN serum = exudate (any one criterion is sufficient); shipbuilding occupational asbestos history with pleural thickening and nodularity makes mesothelioma the priority concern; thoracoscopic or image-guided biopsy is the next step (cytology alone misses about 50% of mesothelioma). (Source: BTS Pleural Disease; ATS Mesothelioma)

options.text: Empyema with pleural pH below 7.20; insert chest drain urgently and treat with IV antibiotics for 2 weeks
options.rationale: Pleural pH 7.34 is above the empyema threshold of 7.20; glucose 4.0 also against empyema (typically <2.2); clinical features and CT findings point to malignancy not infection. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guideline)

options.text: Parapneumonic effusion most likely; commence IV ceftriaxone and review CT pulmonary angiogram for embolus too
options.rationale: No infective history, no fever, normal WCC, glucose and pH within transudate/non-infective range; this is not parapneumonic. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guideline)

options.text: Chylothorax related to thoracic duct injury; arrange lymphangiogram and dietary fat restriction with octreotide
options.rationale: Chylothorax appears milky and triglyceride-rich; straw-coloured fluid excludes this; no history of trauma or thoracic surgery. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guideline)

explanation.summary: Light criteria define exudative pleural effusion when ANY ONE of: pleural/serum protein ratio >0.5; pleural/serum LDH ratio >0.6; pleural LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH. Transudate (failed criteria): heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, hypoalbuminaemia. Exudate: malignancy, infection (parapneumonic, empyema, TB), connective tissue disease, pulmonary embolism, chylothorax, pancreatitis. Additional fluid analysis informs cause: pH <7.20 + glucose <2.2 + LDH >1000 = complicated parapneumonic effusion requiring drainage; pH <7.20 alone in malignant effusion suggests entrapment; ADA >40 U/L supports TB; chyle >1.24 mmol/L triglycerides; amylase raised in pancreatitis and oesophageal rupture. For exudate of unclear cause: cytology (3 large samples, sensitivity 40 to 60%), CT chest (assess parenchyma, pleura, mediastinum), then thoracoscopy or image-guided pleural biopsy (sensitivity >90% for mesothelioma and TB). Occupational asbestos exposure mandates mesothelioma workup. Pleurodesis (talc) or indwelling pleural catheter for recurrent malignant effusion. ANSCO guidelines support multidisciplinary referral for confirmed mesothelioma.
key_points: Light criteria: protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, or pleural LDH >2/3 ULN
Transudate: heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome
Exudate: malignancy, infection, CTD, PE, pancreatitis
Mesothelioma workup: asbestos history + thoracoscopic biopsy
Cytology sensitivity 40-60%; biopsy required for definitive diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Cytology has only 40 to 60% sensitivity for mesothelioma and 60% for adenocarcinoma; thoracoscopic pleural biopsy is the gold standard for both, yielding diagnosis in approximately 95% of cases and allowing simultaneous talc pleurodesis.
